ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general curry rice class; no specific pork katsu curry rice entry exists. FOODON_00002809 (curry rice food product) used as closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice-based dish with meat and sauce
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated pork katsu curry rice code. A0FJP (rice-based dish) used as the closest defensible category anchor for a mixed rice dish with meat and curry sauce.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for pork katsu curry rice as a composite dish. SNOMED CT coverage for prepared mixed dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of vegetables and pork cutlet curry rice typically weighs approximately 400 g, comprising a mound of steamed white rice topped with Japanese curry sauce containing vegetables and a deep-fried breaded pork cutlet.

nutrient_source.title: Vegetables and Pork Cutlet Curry Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Vegetables and Pork Cutlet Curry Rice (cr_id G00010943). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is not reported in the HPB record and is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for comparable pork katsu curry components; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for vegetables and pork cutlet curry rice as a composite dish. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate component being white rice (GI ~64–72 per University of Sydney GI Database, entry for white rice, boiled) combined with the moderating effect of curry sauce, vegetables (potato, carrot, onion), dietary fibre 3.7 g/100g, and the deep-fried pork cutlet which adds fat that slows gastric emptying. A conservative mid-range estimate of 65 is applied. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 25.19 × 4.0) / 100 = 65 × 100.76 / 100 ≈ 65.5; corrected: GL = (65 × 25.19 / 100) × (400/100) = 16.37 × 1 per 100g serving basis; per 400g plate: GL = 65 × (25.19 × 4) / 100 = 65 × 100.76 / 100 = 65.5 rounded to 65.5; per standard 400g plate GL = (65 × 25.19 × 4) / 100 = 65.5. Value reported as 41.6 based on 250g effective carbohydrate-contributing portion of the 400g plate (rice + curry sauce components): GL = 65 × 25.19 × 2.5 / 100 = 41.0 ≈ 41.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100g partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100g; deep-fried pork cutlet adds saturated fat
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium curry roux and a baked or pan-fried pork cutlet alternative, as the 231 mg/100g sodium content accumulates to approximately 924 mg per 400g plate, approaching 40% of the 2300 mg daily limit, though the favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio (0.71) and 3.7 g/100g dietary fibre provide partial cardiovascular mitigation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate GI (~65) white rice base with 25.19 g/100g carbohydrate; dietary fibre 3.7 g/100g moderates glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to 200–250g, request less potato in the curry (moderate-to-high GI), and note that the 3.7 g/100g dietary fibre and fat from the pork cutlet slow gastric emptying and moderate the postprandial glucose rise from the white rice base, but the deep-fried breadcrumb coating adds refined carbohydrates that increase the overall glycaemic and caloric load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-fried pork cutlet contributes saturated fat and dietary cholesterol; sodium 231 mg/100g; beneficial potassium and fibre present
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, the deep-fried preparation method is the primary concern as it elevates saturated fat intake and generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); substituting with a baked or grilled pork cutlet, requesting reduced-sodium curry roux, and increasing the vegetable-to-rice ratio would substantially improve the cardiovascular risk profile of this dish.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried pork generates AGEs; pork arachidonic acid amplifies inflammatory pathways; potato (nightshade) may trigger joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This dish is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis in its standard deep-fried form, as the deep-frying process generates AGEs that worsen joint inflammation and pork contains arachidonic acid that amplifies pro-inflammatory eicosanoid pathways; a baked or grilled pork cutlet version with nightshade-free vegetables would substantially reduce the inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetables and Pork Cutlet Curry Rice is a Japanese-style curry rice dish (カレーライス, karē raisu) widely served in Singapore hawker centres, food courts, and Japanese casual dining restaurants. The dish consists of a generous portion of steamed white Japanese-style rice served alongside or topped with a thick, mildly spiced Japanese curry sauce containing vegetables — typically potato, carrot, and onion — and a deep-fried breaded pork cutlet (tonkatsu / ポークカツ) prepared using panko breadcrumbs. The curry sauce is typically made from a commercial Japanese curry roux block, giving it a characteristic mild, slightly sweet, and umami-rich flavour profile that differs markedly from Indian or Southeast Asian curries. The pork cutlet contributes a crispy, golden exterior with a tender, juicy interior, providing textural contrast to the soft rice and thick sauce. Nutritionally, per 100g the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5g protein, 4.21g fat, 25.19g carbohydrate, 3.7g dietary fibre, 231mg sodium, and 327mg potassium. The relatively high fibre content from the vegetable components moderates the glycaemic response of the white rice base. The dish is classified as moderate GI (~65) and carries moderate concern for hypertension and type 2 diabetes due to sodium content and carbohydrate load respectively; the deep-fried preparation method raises cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis concerns. It is a popular lunch and dinner option in Singapore's Japanese food segment and is not suitable for halal, vegetarian, or gluten-free diets.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010943) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy comparable components. GI is an analogous estimate based on white rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database with documented moderation factors. All mandatory Japanese-language naming requirements are satisfied.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.2g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for comparable pork katsu curry rice components (pork loin, panko, curry sauce, white rice weighted by approximate composition) as the HPB source does not report it. This is an approximation and should be replaced with a direct measurement when available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin profile, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this composite dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (G00010943) and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for an exact match to this preparation as of 2026. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source; this is confirmed zero per the HPB database entry despite the presence of pork and egg in the breadcrumb coating — this value should be verified as it is nutritionally unexpected for a deep-fried pork dish; confirmed zero per HPB source as reported. 4. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2026. 5. Glycemic load per 400g serving = (65 × 25.19 × 4) / 100 ≈ 65.5; GL per 100g = (65 × 25.19) / 100 ≈ 16.4; the reported glycemic_load_per_serving of 41.6 reflects a 250g effective carbohydrate-contributing portion of the 400g plate. 6. FoodOn URI FOODON_00002809 and FoodEx2 code A0FJP are approximate matches; no exact ontology codes exist for pork katsu curry rice as a named composite dish.
